text:
Team GBhave eclipsed their medal target for theParis Olympic Gamesand even bagged more they did at Tokyo four years ago.

UK Sport originally issued a target between 50 and 70 medals for British athletes to claim this summer and they were aiming to be amongst the top five medal-winning nations. With the sun now having set on a dramatic two weeks in Paris, Team GB actuallyclaimed 65 medals, however they had to settle for seventh in the overall medal table due to a drop in gold medals.

Securing 14 golds, 22 silvers and 29 bronze medals overall, the Brits actually beat their target of 50 on Day 12 of the Games. Despite exceeding their goals, Team GB's gold medal total is their lowest since they returned just nine at the 2004 Olympics in Athens.

Great Britain's highest gold medal total was 29 in 2012, while65 medals were won in total in London. The team claimed a record 67 medals in Rio four years later while the Tokyo Olympics saw Team GB take 64 with 22 gold, 20 silver and 22 bronze medals.

Before the games,a statement on the UK Sport website read: "For the Paris 2024 Olympic Games we are hoping for sports to cumulatively win between 50 and 70 medals, while for the Paralympic Games the range is between 100 and 140 medals. Our ambition remains for Team GB and Paralympics GB to secure a top-five position in the medal table at both Games."

Despite missing out at the higher end ofthe medal charts, Paris saw Team GB actually clinch their 1,000th medal across Olympic and Winter Olympic Games. Ethan Hayter, Daniel Bigham, Charlie Tanfield and Ethan Vernon secured a silver medal in the Cycling Men’s Team Pursuit on August 7th to cement the honour for Great Britain.

Measured by how many golds a nation captures, China finished top of the medal charts with 40 golds and 91 medals overall while the USA came second with 38 golds yet sealed an astonishing 125 medals overall. Japan came in third place with20 golds and 45 medals in general, Australia secured fourth thanks to 18 golds and 53 medals altogether while hosts France won 16 golds and 63 medals on the whole.



Making up Team GB's gold medal winners are Tom McEwen, Ros Canter, and Laura Collett, who won it in the Equestrian while Ben Maher, Scott Brash and Harry Charles also came out on top in the event.

Tom Pidcock won first prize for Mountain Bikingwhile Nathan Hales showed off his skills to clinch gold in Shooting. James Guy, Tom Dean, Matt Richards, and Duncan Scott shone in the Men's 4x200m Freestyle swim to capture gold whileAlex Yeecame on top of the podium in the Men's Triathlon.

Lauren Henry, Hannah Scott, Lola Anderson and Georgie Brayshaw did Team GB proud in the Women's Quadruple Sculls, Jacob Dawson, Charlie Elwes, James Rudkin, Tom Ford, Sholto Carnegie, Rory Gibbs, Morgan Bolding, Tom Digby and Harry Brightmore also bagged gold for Rowing, while on dry landBryony Page secured goldon the Trampoline.

Katy Marchant, Sophie Capewell and Emma Finucane claimed glory in Cycling, whileKeely Hodgkinson, Women's 800m, Ellie Aldridge, for Sailing, and Toby Roberts for Climbing all took home the biggest prize of all.



Away from the golds, Team GB saw silver and bronze medal glory thanks to athletes such as Josh Kerr who came runner up in the Men's 1500m, swimming legend Adam Peaty who took silver in the Men's 100m Breaststroke,Tom Daley and Noah Williams who got their silver medalin the Men's Synchronised 10m Platform dive andMatt Hudson-Smithwho agonisingly just missed out on gold in the Men's 400m final.

Dina Asher-Smith, Imani Lansiquot, Amy Hunt and Daryll Neita also took silver in the 4x100m Women's Relay while Katarina Johnson-Thompson had to settle for silver in the Women's Heptathlon after missing out to triple Olympic champion Nafissatou Thiam.

When it comes to bronze, Team GB scored the second highest of the third place medal just behind Team USA thanks to athletes such asAndrea Spendolini-Sirieixand Lois Toulson, who won the medal in the Women's Synchronised 10m Platform diving and Jack Laugher and Anthony Harding, who also won in synchronised diving.

16-year-old Sky Brownwon her second Bronze at her second Olympics in Women's Skateboarding while Jeremiah Azu, Louie Hinchliffe, Nethaneel Mitchell-Blake and Zharnel Hughes also earned the medal in the Men's 4x100m Relay.

title: How many Olympic medals Team GB achieved compared to target set | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Chris Burns
description: The Olympic torch has now been passed on to Los Angeles for 2028 as Team GB look back on an impressive medal haul in Paris
